title: Exploring Altered Open Source Software Development Patterns in a Time of Generative AI

study_design: We leverage the March 2023 announcement and April 2023 public release of GitHub Copilot X (CX) as a natural experiment, analyzing a 27-week user-week panel of 1,350 GitHub developers using difference-in-differences with coarsened exact matching.
We further augment our GitHub data with matched LinkedIn career trajectories over the subsequent 6–12 months.

Using an intent-to-treat difference-in-differences (DID) empirical setting around CX's public release, we find treated developers exhibit productivity gains in both commits (≈12% increase) and active repositories (≈6% increase) compared to the control group.
We estimate the intent-to-treat effects of ACAs on users' development activities and work patterns through a two-way fixed effects difference-in-differences (DID) specification.
Consistent with our identification strategy, we separate the post-CX period into two analytically distinct treatment phases.
We employ a snowball sampling strategy that began with the five most popular GitHub users and expanded through their starred-user networks, yielding a final sample of 4,021 developers.
Mirroring CX's staged release, we created a user-week panel that spans 27 weeks: 12 weeks prior to the announcement, a 5-week pre-launch promotion/registration period, and 10 weeks post-launch.
To mitigate this self-selection bias, we construct comparable user pairs by matching on key characteristics and pre-treatment programming activities on GitHub.
We apply coarsened exact matching (CEM) using all observations, weighting control users by the ratio of treated to control units within each stratum.
This procedure yields a matched sample of 1,373 users–567 treated and 806 controls–with stratum-specific weights carried forward into the subsequent DID analyses.
task: GitHub developers advance their projects through an iterative process comprising local code development and subsequent submission of modifications as commits to remote repositories.
Within this iterative cycle, developers generate ideas for new features or improvements to the existing codebase, then implement these ideas through programming.

The coding phase—historically the most cognitively demanding—requires developers to understand existing code architecture, recall syntax and language conventions, consult documentation, and write new code that integrates with the existing codebase.
Once satisfied with their changes, developers commit these modifications locally before pushing them to the remote repository, making their work visible to the broader community.
External contributors who lack direct write access may propose changes through pull requests, which project maintainers review and potentially merge.

This cycle of ideation, implementation, commitment, and collaboration repeats continuously as the project evolves, with developers regularly returning to add features, fix bugs, or refactor existing code within the same repository.
population: We employed a snowball sampling strategy that began with the five most popular GitHub users and expanded through their starred-user networks, yielding a final sample of 4,021 developers.
Users are retained if they contribute at least one commit in both the pre-announcement and any of two post-announcement windows.

Our measurement construction process yields an analytical sample of 1,350 users for subsequent empirical analyses; of the original 1,373 matched users, 23 are excluded due to incomplete data coverage for either the focal user or their matched counterpart.
Table 1 presents descriptive statistics for our primary analytical variables across these 1,350 users, encompassing 36,450 user-week observations in the panel structure.

Upon inspecting the distributional properties, we find considerable heterogeneity in weekly development engagement: developers average 1.93 commits per week and actively contribute to 0.37 repositories.

We manually match users across platforms by systematically searching public LinkedIn profiles using identifying information from GitHub accounts, including username keywords and cross-platform social media links. After rigorous identity verification using available biographical information, we successfully matched 2,978 users from our full sample to verified LinkedIn profiles.
Based on these constructed employment-related variables, we develop a cross-sectional analytical dataset comprising 470 developers from our matched sample.
arms: Under the assumption that Pro plan subscribers are more likely Copilot users, we leverage users' pro status to construct treatment and control groups.
The first encompasses the post-announcement phase, during which enrollment occurred but the majority of users remained without functional CX access.
The second phase captures the post-launch period, characterized by broad availability of CX's advanced ACA chat functionalities across the treatment population.
Pro i denotes useri's Pro subscription status at the announcement date.

PostAnnouncement t is a time-varying indicator equaling one for weeks following GitHub's public announcement of CX and its forthcoming AI-assisted development vision, but preceding the availability of enhanced Chat features through IDE marketplaces, while PostLaunch t equals one during and after the official release of the chat-based ACA extension.
outcome_definitions: We first operationalize development intensity through two primary metrics: each user's weekly commit frequency and the count of concurrently active projects (repositories).

While the frequency of commits during a given temporal window provides a general proxy for development effort, a more granular analysis of modifications between consecutive commits allows for richer analytical insights into the nature and magnitude of developmental work.
We quantify both the scale and substantive character of developers' weekly contributions, enabling an in-depth assessment of coding productivity and behavioral patterns.

We implement a filtering mechanism that excludes non-programming file modifications and systematically removes auto-generated code patterns and repetitive line sequences, thereby constructing a meaningful line changes metric that isolates substantive developmental contributions.
We classify each line-level operation (i.e., additions, deletions, modifications, and relocation) to characterize developers' weekly editing behaviors.

Building upon this classification, we follow GitClear's proprietary indicators to help operationalize their metric diff delta, which quantifies coding effort magnitude through weighted scores calibrated to the estimated cognitive load of each operation type.

Another measure of interest, code churn, was captured to show the proportion of code subsequently revised or removed within a specified temporal window, serving as an inverse proxy for code stability.

We operationalize a user's baseline programming breadth by enumerating the unique programming languages present across owned repositories, subsequently tracking the adoption of novel programming languages within submitted code contributions on a weekly basis.

To capture project-level exploration behaviors, we construct a user-specific repository novelty measure whereby we extract the project's textual data, including the README documentation for each newly created repository.

We utilize Natural Language Processing to create semantic text embeddings so that we could compute the mean cosine distance between each new repository's semantic embedding and the centroid of embeddings derived from the user's historical project portfolio (we utilized the preceding twelve-month period from the observational period).
Code Churn % - Percentage of code added in the week that is modified or removed within the subsequent 2 weeks
Copy-pasted % - Percentage of code that appears to be copy-pasted (e.g., duplicated blocks, low edit distance with external sources, or repeated snippets within the repo)

Project Novelty - Mean cosine distance between embeddings of repository descriptions for repos created in the week and embeddings of the developer's existing repos (repos created within the 12 months prior to the observation window)
Languages Added - Number of distinct programming languages first observed in a given week (relative to all previously seen languages)
Sessions - Average number of commit-activity clusters in a given week; a new session begins after≥4 hours of inactivity
Session Duration - Average duration (in minutes) of sessions within the week
Session Intermission - Average gap (in hours) between consecutive sessions
Work Day Dist. Std. - Standard deviation of the distribution of work sessions within a week
Weekend Commit % - Share of commits that occur on Saturday or Sunday
statistical_results: We find that treated developers increase commit frequency by approximately 12% and active repositories by 6%.

Beyond volume, work patterns shift toward broader engagement, with casual developers driving a 48% increase in work hours and increasing their exploration of new programming languages, while active developers show no significant increase in time input.
However, this expansion comes with trade-offs in code quality, as we document a 2 percentage-point increase in copy-pasted code and a decline in project novelty for casual users.
Finally, we find that AI-enabled shifts in development behavior are associated with a higher likelihood of internal promotions in the short- to medium-term.

Column 1 reveals that the CX announcement is associated with a statistically significant increase (δ1 =0.1, p-value<0.01) in the weekly commit frequency among Pro users, relative to the control group.

Following the public release of CX chat features on the IDE marketplaces, this positive treatment effect on commit volume demonstrates persistence, with the absolute marginal difference expanding from 0.16 to 0.21 commits per week.

Upon incorporating controls for average project lifecycle stage and cumulative developer experience in column 2, the estimated treatment effects weaken modestly yet retain statistical significance (δ 1 =0.09, p-value<0.05;δ 2 =0.12, p-value<0.01).

When compared against the sample mean baseline of 0.5 weekly commits, the coefficients in column 2 corresponds to a proportional increase of approximately 28% and 34% during the announcement and post-release phase, respectively.

On average, treated users contributed to 0.08 additional repositories per week relative to their outcomes absent CX access, representing a 35% proportional increase in active project portfolios compared to the control cohort.
While modest in absolute terms, this relative increase is sizable against a baseline mean of 0.23 active repositories.
The estimation results demonstrate that commits submitted by Pro users encompass more extensive code modifications following the full availability of CX features on IDE marketplaces (columns 1-3).

Specifically, the incremental increase in line-level modifications within an average commit exhibits greater magnitude among Pro users relative to non-Pro users across both post-treatment temporal phases.

While the announcement phase yields statistically insignificant treatment effects, the post-launch phase demonstrates a statistically significant effect whereby we observe a 32% proportional increase in line changes among Pro users, indicating that the operational deployment of CX capabilities corresponded to meaningful shifts in per-commit code contributions and intensity.

Beyond raw lines of code (LOC) changes, which systematically underestimate true cognitive coding effort (Shihab et al., 2013), meaningful line changes (column 2) exhibit stronger ITT effects that intensify substantially following the public deployment of CX's enhanced capabilities to authorized users.

A parallel pattern emerges for Diff Delta, an empirically validated developer effort metric that assigns operation-specific weighted scores to classified edit operations captured within meaningful line changes.

The increased magnitude and heightened statistical significance of treatment effects for meaningful line changes and Diff Delta collectively indicate that CX primarily augments developers' substantive coding activities, facilitating the production of more usable and semantically meaningful code modifications rather than superficial or low-signal edits such as whitespace formatting, documentation comments, or extensive string literal assignments.
In contrast, column 4 demonstrates no statistically significant treatment effect at the file-volume level.
The estimation results reveal no statistically significant increase in users' weekly time allocation following the full deployment of CX capabilities.

A temporary elevation in working hours emerges during the announcement phase (column 1), yet this effect appears predominantly attributable to an increased frequency of session initiations (column 2) rather than extended session durations (column 3).

This short-lived pattern aligns with brief exploratory behavior whereby developers experiment with newly announced capabilities amid broader enthusiasm surrounding generative AI tool proliferation, rather than reflecting systematic intensification of working hours.

Upon looking at the post-launch phase, coefficient estimates across all three specifications indicate negligible magnitudes in time allocation changes, both at the aggregate weekly level and at the per-session granularity.
Collectively, these empirical findings suggest that the documented output expansion cannot be attributed to heightened time investment into development activities.
Instead, the evidence indicates that ACAs effectively reduce the marginal cost of code production while simultaneously enhancing per-unit productivity.

This productivity shift enables developers to accomplish substantively greater work volumes within approximately equivalent temporal windows during active development weeks, suggesting efficient reallocation of cognitive resources toward more meaningful coding activities.
Column 1 demonstrates that average intermission duration between consecutive sessions increases substantially.

When considered alongside our prior finding that CX users actively contribute to significantly more repositories, these extended intermissions may reflect project-level multitasking behavior: developers distribute their constrained temporal budgets more sparsely across parallel project portfolios rather than concentrating effort within continuous blocks devoted to individual initiatives.
Column 2 provides additional support, demonstrating elevated standard deviation in daily activity levels that indicates greater temporal fragmentation across the weekly cycle.

These patterns align with emerging evidence that generative AI capabilities may attenuate cognitive friction in context switching (Brynjolfsson et al., 2025), as CX features including contextual code summarization and natural-language interaction effectively reduce the overhead of resuming dormant projects.

Critically, this temporal restructuring operates predominantly within developers' pre-existing schedules rather than expanding into discretionary leisure periods, as column 3 demonstrates no pronounced treatment effect on weekend contribution patterns among average Pro users.

Nevertheless, subsequent heterogeneity analyses reveal that developers exhibiting historically sparse contribution patterns demonstrate substantively different behavioral responses, initiating more frequent sessions and increasingly utilizing weekend periods for expanded project engagement (see Tables 7 and A3).
Column 1 of Table 6 presents evidence that CX deployment substantively increases programming language experimentation.

Employing a Poisson specification appropriate for count data with low-frequency outcomes, we estimate that CX launch corresponds to a 68% increase (e 0.52 −1) in the rate of novel programming language adoption among Pro users relative to control developers.

This finding aligns with the theoretical expectation that natural language mediation capabilities reduce the cognitive overhead traditionally required to produce syntactically valid code in unfamiliar linguistic environments.

By enabling developers to leverage CX's retrieval and code synthesis functionalities rather than relying exclusively on memorized syntax and language-specific conventions, these capabilities effectively lower barriers to technical diversification.

Synthesizing this result with the documented temporal restructuring of development activity, the evidence indicates that CX primarily expands the exploration potential rather than development quality.
In sum, developers maintain approximately constant temporal investments while distributing their fixed time budgets across wider project portfolios and more diverse technical environments.
We first assess short-run stability of development output using Code Churn % (Column 2, Table 6).
As a proxy for near-term rework intensity, Code Churn % shows no statistically significant change in the post-announcement period, and the estimated effect becomes modestly positive after launch.
These estimates suggest that CX does not immediately disrupt developers' development processes, but neither does it materially dampen short-term rework volatility.
We next examine medium-run structural effects.
Column 3 indicates that CX adoption is associated with a persistent increase in code redundancy.
The coefficient on Copy-pasted % is approximately 0.02 in both periods, implying an increase of about two percentage points in duplicated lines.
This pattern is consistent with an AI-augmented workflow that relies more heavily on reusable templates and repeated implementation patterns.
While such repetition can accelerate feature delivery, it may also introduce maintenance burdens by weakening abstraction and increase downstream refactoring costs (Haefliger et al., 2008).
Finally, we test whether these efficiency-oriented shifts translate into greater innovative distinctiveness using Project Novelty (Column 4, Table 6).

For each new repository, we assemble project text (including README documentation) and compute semantic similarity to the developer's pre-observation portfolio; we define novelty as the inverse of this similarity.
Column 4 shows no aggregate effect, indicating that expansion in activity does not automatically translate into more distinctive project themes.

Heterogeneity results (Column 6, Table 7) further suggest a small decline in novelty among previously less active users, consistent with greater reliance on default AI suggestions when initiating new projects.
Taken together, the results in Table 6 reveal a tension between development exploration and quality within AI-augmented workflows.

Along the exploration dimension, captured by technical scope expansion through novel programming language adoption (column 1), CX enables developers to distribute effort across broadened technical environments alongside the temporal fragmentation patterns documented previously.
Along the quality dimension, however, we observe concerning patterns.

Technical quality indicators reveal statistically insignificant changes in code churn (column 2) and significant increases in code redundancy (column 3), suggesting that AI-augmented development introduces maintainability challenges through duplicated logic.
Furthermore, project novelty remains unaffected by the intervention (column 4), indicating that CX does not enhance developers' capacity to initiate genuinely innovative projects.

These asymmetric effects suggest that while CX effectively reduces cognitive switching costs associated with context resumption and initiating work in unfamiliar technical domains, the tool does not substantively relax the architectural reasoning and creative ideation constraints that govern code maintainability and innovative output generation.

Table 6 thus establishes empirically grounded boundary conditions for ACA-enabled productivity gains: within the observed temporal window, CX predominantly expands the exploration of development activity while simultaneously introducing technical debt through decreased code quality, with innovation outcomes remaining effectively unchanged by the intervention.
For casual users, CX introduction serves as a catalyst for sustained participation increases.
As shown in columns 1-4, these Pro users exhibit persistent and statistically significant elevations in development activity during both post-announcement and post-launch periods.
The temporal patterns further reveal that during the post-announcement period, the majority of users experience increases in both activity levels and time investment.
However, this broadly distributed increase gradually attenuates for Pro developers with stable development habits.
This pattern suggests both heightened public interest following the announcement and a differential motivating effect of CX that disproportionately benefits the casual developer group.

Most notably, while the average treatment effect on working duration remained non-significant in the post-launch period (Table 4), casual users experienced substantial increases in time investment (47.2%)–both their session initiation volume (column 4 of Table 7) and session duration (column 1 of Table A3) rose significantly.
This effect substantially diminishes and reverses for the most active users, who contributed similar volumes of meaningful code (column 2 of Table A2) while working fewer hours (column 3 of Table 7).
Additional analysis in Appendix 5 confirms that distributed work patterns documented earlier primarily occurred among users with more casual users (Table A3).
These asymmetric responses reveal that CX fundamentally alters participation dynamics for casual contributors.
By reducing cognitive barriers to code comprehension and production, CX enables exploratory engagement patterns previously inaccessible to inexperienced developers.

The documented increases in both session frequency and duration among these users suggest that ACAs effectively lower technical entry thresholds, transforming OSS development from an activity requiring sustained commitment into one accommodating intermittent, learning-oriented participation.
We also observe more pronounced effects on new language adoption among casual users (column 5), while CX appears to dampen language exploration among experienced users.
This asymmetric pattern aligns with the effort-saving behavior documented earlier for experienced developers.
In contrast, casual users exhibit declining project novelty (column 6).

This pattern may appear inconsistent with existing evidence that inexperienced developers typically join projects requiring different technical expertise and derive higher learning benefits from diverse exposures (Hahn et al., 2008).

However, because our study examines only users' activities within their own repositories, casual developers likely continue pursuing external learning through collaborative participation in others' projects, where knowledge exchange and communication with experienced contributors remain valuable development pathways.

The simultaneous decline in project novelty alongside expanded language adoption suggests that newly incorporated technical components serve primarily auxiliary functions rather than representing fundamental architectural shifts.
While CX substantially enriches casual users' language portfolios, it does not appear to catalyze genuinely disruptive innovation efforts beyond their established technical domains.

Exploratory experimentation predominantly occurs at the boundary of existing capabilities, where ACAs' inherent limitations in creative ideation constrain their capacity to overcome developers' path-dependent trajectories.

Overall, the estimated coefficients of the "leads" (i.e., weeks in the pre-treatment periods) are rarely statistically insignificant at the 5% level, indicating no significant differences between treatment and control groups across pretreatment weeks.
A joint test of all pretreatment coefficients fails to reject the null of no systematic difference between treatment and control groups prior to CX exposure.

Although the coefficients for weeks pre6 and pre7 are significantly negative in one specification, a joint test of all pretreatment coefficients fails to reject the null of no systematic difference between treatment and control groups prior to CX exposure.
After matching, standardized mean differences across covariates are generally below 0.2, indicating satisfactory balance between the treatment and control groups (see Figure 3).

We further assess robustness using alternative matching approaches, including propensity score matching (PSM), and by expanding the matching set to incorporate users' historical development patterns (e.g., maintenance work ratio and work cadence), which we report in later sections.
This richer coarsening scheme tightens the balance on coding intensity, persistence, and demographics, thereby further limiting unobserved heterogeneity relevant to Pro subscription decisions.
Results based on this stricter CEM sample in Table A5 remain qualitatively consistent with and close in magnitude to our main estimates.
We also employ PSM as an alternative to CEM to address concerns about our matching method selection.
Specifically, we conduct one-to-one, without-replacement matching using the same set of covariates that predict both Pro plan subscription and our outcomes variables.
This specification yields 529 matched pairs (1,058 users total).
Analysis of this alternative matched sample corroborates our main findings, as shown in Table A6.
Our results show significant increases in development activity at both the commit and repository levels.
Although effect magnitudes are somewhat smaller than in our main analysis, the consistency in this alternative PSM specification supports the robustness of our primary conclusions.
Table 9 examines how ACA access affects career trajectories through Probit regression analysis of job transitions.
We categorize transitions into external job hopping (employer changes) and internal transitions, encompassing both lateral transfers and hierarchical promotions.
The estimation results reveal that ACA access significantly enhances career advancement, operating primarily through internal mobility channels.

Pro users show higher probabilities of internal transitions (coefficient = 0.5, p-value<0.05) in the first 6 months, with a consistently strong association (coefficient = 0.46, p-value<0.01) extending to 12 months.
Within these internal moves, we observe substantial promotion effects (coefficient = 0.46, p-value<0.05) in the short term.

The longer-term analysis reveals persistent associations with internal mobility, though the promotion effect moderates somewhat (coefficient = 0.31, p-value<0.1), potentially reflecting broader organizational adoption of AI development tools that diminishes relative advantages.
Notably, we detect no statistically significant relationship with external job hopping, indicating that career benefits materialize predominantly through internal advancement pathways.

This pattern exhibits theoretical coherence with our earlier empirical findings that AI-enabled productivity gains and technical experimentation concentrate primarily within personal rather than community repositories, rendering performance improvements more readily observable to current employers than to external labor markets.

Moreover, because private organizational repositories remain unobservable in our data, the stronger internal mobility associations may partially reflect AI-enhanced contributions within firms' proprietary codebases that inform promotion decisions.

While these results demonstrate that CX access correlates positively with enhanced internal career mobility, the precise causal mechanism (whether operating through enahnced signaling value of observable OSS participation or through accelerated skill acquisition enabling superior task performance) remains an open empirical question requiring further investigation.
We find that access to CX is associated with higher development intensity and broader engagement.
Treated developers increase commit frequency by approximately 12% and active repositories by about 6% relative to controls, while meaningful line changes per commit rise by roughly 63%.
Intermissions between sessions also lengthen, consistent with lower context-switching costs and more flexible allocation of effort across repositories and files.

These effects are highly heterogeneous: the increase in work hours (around 48%) and in new programming-language adoption is concentrated among previously casual developers, whereas highly active developers show no significant change in time spent.

This pattern is consistent with a participation-expansion mechanism among casual contributors, complementing Kreitmeir and Raschky (2024) and contrasting with Song et al. (2023), who document greater usage among core developers for the initial Copilot release.

One interpretation is that CX's conversational interface and multi-file context reduce information-seeking and switching costs that previously constrained more casual users (Song et al., 2023; Yeverechyahu et al., 2024).
These participation gains have important boundary conditions for code quality and innovation.
We observe an increase of roughly two percentage points in copy-pasted code, indicating greater reliance on duplicated or template-like solutions.
Project novelty declines significantly for casual developers, while highly active developers show no significant change in novelty within our observation window.
These patterns differ from Yeverechyahu et al., 2024, who document code-quality improvements among high-quality programmers using earlier Copilot versions.

A plausible explanation is compositional: whereas Yeverechyahu et al., 2024 study a setting in which adoption was concentrated among technically sophisticated users who could integrate inline completion into optimized workflows, CX's conversational interface lowers adoption barriers and expands participation among less active developers who previously faced higher entry costs.
Our quality findings therefore likely reflect shifts in the active developer pool rather than degradation among equivalent users.

The increase in copy-pasted code and reduced novelty among casual contributors suggest that CX democratizes access faster than it cultivates the architectural judgment and design discipline developed through extensive practice.

Overall, CX broadens participation and raises visible output for less active developers, while the cognitive and architectural demands of producing distinct, high-novelty innovations remain harder to support, generating an exploration–quality trade-off in ACA-supported development.
We also find that ACA access is associated with near-term career benefits.
Linking GitHub activity to LinkedIn data, we observe higher rates of internal transitions and promotions within 6–12 months after CX's release.
This early evidence suggests a potential shift in the timing of OSS-related career returns.

Distinct from the long-horizon external signaling emphasized in prior work (Hann et al., 2013; Lerner & Tirole, 2002), we posit that visible engagement with advanced AI tools may enable faster, within-firm rewards.

Mediation analysis indicates that neither raw commit volume nor other observable development contribution changes account for these gains, consistent with the idea that, in labor markets where AI literacy is an emerging credential, visible AI tool adoption functions as a direct signal of adaptability and technical currency rather than merely a channel for higher observable output.

Additional heterogeneity evidence suggests that this effect is even more pronounced for interns and junior employees, who often rely on such technical indicators to overcome a lack of professional history.

This skill-signaling interpretation aligns with evidence that visibility of developers' technical skills on GitHub is associated with promotions (Daniel et al., 2025), but we cannot observe employer decision processes or the specific attributes they reward.

Accordingly, we treat signaling as a plausible–but not empirically verified–mechanism, and open questions remain about the durability of these benefits and their interaction with evolving organizational norms around AI-assisted contributions.
By reviewing Copilot-related topics, we find that 436 out of 1,634 participants (26.7%) are Pro users, more than double the proportion found in GitHub's active user population.
This large magnitude difference suggests that Copilot use is much more prevalent among Pro users at the time of CXs' launch.
We trained a bespoke Deep Learning model to predict the likelihood of new code addition being either human or AI generated.

Statistical testing verified that Pro (compared to non-Pro and within Pro status pre vs. post CX) users showed a significantly higher proclivity of having AI-generated code additions post CX (full details can be found in Appendix 7).

Taken together, these two pieces of evidence support the validity of our ITT approach by showing that, in light of prior findings, holding a Pro subscription is a strong proxy for CX adoption and use.

In the Pre-Launch period, Pro users showed a higher propensity for AI commits (β 1 = 0.0955), but this difference was not statistically significant (p= 0.144), suggesting a comparable baseline between groups.
However, in the Post-Launch period, the gap widened (β 1 = 0.1030) and became statistically significant (p= 0.035).

Furthermore, the longitudinal analysis confirms that Pro users significantly increased their AI adoption rate by approximately 3.75 percentage points (α1 = 0.0375, p= 0.011) following the release of Copilot X tools.
The analysis revealed a statistically significant main effect of experience (F(1,710) = 3.92,p=.048).

As illustrated in Figure A6, casual developers exhibited significantly higher sentiment (estimated marginal mean = 0.20) compared to active developers (estimated marginal mean = 0.13) when discussing Copilot.
We find statistically insignificant results across all six outcome variables.
This indicates that student status does not significantly moderate the treatment effect.
The productivity gains associated with CX adoption are evident for both student and non-student users, confirming that our main results are not an artifact of the student population.

The results reveal that ACA adoption significantly increases push frequency and size to Personal Projects (columns 2 and 5), while showing no significant effects on contributions to Collaborative Projects (columns 3 and 6).
This pattern suggests that developers primarily leverage ACAs for their own projects rather than for external collaborative work.
As complementary evidence, we examine whether the benefits of ACAs vary by a developer's overall propensity for collaborative development.

We defined OSS Proclivity as the percentage of a user's repositories that have received at least one external author contribution, serving as a proxy for how deeply a user is integrated into the collaborative OSS ecosystem.
Table A15 presents heterogeneous treatment effects by OSS Proclivity.
The interaction terms are largely statistically insignificant across most outcomes, suggesting that the observed treatment effects do not meaningfully vary by a developer's collaborative orientation.

Taken together, these analyses indicate that while ACAs primarily enhance activity on personal projects, their benefits appear broadly applicable regardless of whether developers typically work in collaborative or solo contexts.
We conducted a Poisson regression analysis at the monthly level because new project launches occur infrequently at the weekly level, making it difficult to draw meaningful statistical conclusions.
Result in A16 highlights a transient significant rise in new project creation during the announcement stage, coinciding with a rise in public interest in Gen-AI projects.
However, the effect becomes insignificant at a longer time horizon.

Combined with the persistent rise in commit volume and worked-on repositories, these findings support that ACAs primarily lead to persistent development activity enhancement, rather than one-off project initiations.
Our findings revealed a consistent and increasing trend in commit activities when using all the self-owned repositories.

This eliminates the alternative explanation that the rise in development activities is primarily due to Pro users being particularly interested in generative-AI-related development during that specific period.
We found a consistent rise in the number of new programming language adopted in treated users' development at the post-launch stage.
Columns (1) and (4) report the results of linear regression for the mediator model (M∼T+X).
Columns (2), (3), (5), and (6) report the results of probit models for the outcome models (Y∼T+X) and Y∼T+M+X).
Our findings in Tables A21 and A22 suggest that the rise in activity observed after CX introduction is unlikely to be the mechanism driving users' internal position changes.
Specifically, we find no evidence that commit volume mediates promotion outcomes.

Although the treatment coefficient decreases marginally between Columns 2 and 3 in Table A21, the effect of the mediator on the outcome is statistically insignificant, indicating that increased output volume does not predict promotions.
Additionally, we observe that the impact of the treatment on the mediator (commit volume) fades and becomes insignificant over the longer (12-month) time span.
Collectively, these results imply that raw commit volume is not the channel through which CX access improves career outcomes.

Instead, the positive association is likely driven by direct effects, suggesting that AI usage may act as a quality signal to employers or drives efficiency gains not captured by simple commit counts.
We found no significant difference in this association between student and non-student users (Panel A).

However, we observed heterogeneous associations for developers with varying employment contracts (Panel B) and work experience (Panel C) over a 12-month horizon: 1) ACA usage among interns is associated with significantly greater internal progression opportunities (internal changes and promotions) compared to other contract types (columns 5-6). 2) Senior employees with more prior work experience appear less likely to leverage ACA usage for external job hopping (column 4), as indicated by the negative interaction term.
ambiguity_or_caveats: While the subscription status of Copilot is not publicly shown on users' profiles, we can easily observe if a user has subscribed to the GitHub Pro plan, a premium service on GitHub.

Here, we argue that users' subscription statuses of this paid service can be considered a good proxy of treatment in our intention-to-treat (ITT) analyses, given that this premium service subscription is commonly bundled with a GitHub Copilot subscription.
This strong binding relationship between the two services can be justified for two reasons.

First, educational users can apply for access to these two paid services as a bundle for free through the GitHub Education scheme, where an educational developer pack covering these two services is provided to all verified educational users (both students and faculty).
Second, complementary purchases frequently occur in organizations' software service procurement due to switching costs and established customer relationships (Chen & Hitt, 2006).

Software providers' cross-selling strategies and sequential marketing campaigns, particularly through automated email promotions to existing subscribers, further increase customers' likelihood of adopting complementary products and services (Liu-Thompkins & Tam, 2013).

Because the API exposes subscription status only at the time of data collection, we additionally verified timing using the Wayback Machine to confirm whether each user's Pro subscription began before the CX release.
This review excluded 900 users who obtained Pro status immediately after our observation window and revealed a large cohort joining Pro in the first three months following the CX roll-out.
Since developers self-selected into this premium plan, Pro users' characteristics may differ substantially from non-Pro users.
To mitigate this self-selection bias, we construct comparable user pairs by matching on key characteristics and pre-treatment programming activities on GitHub.

Nevertheless, constructing valid and reliable measures of workload intensity and code quality in software development contexts presents substantial methodological challenges, attributable to considerable heterogeneity in coding conventions and marked variations in the complexity of developers' contributions.
Moreover, comprehensive analysis of granular diff data necessitates significant computational resources to effectively parse extensive modification files when executing raw code analytics at scale.

Consequently, prior empirical investigations have predominantly employed coarse-grained proxies—including commit counts, pull request frequencies, or aggregate lines of code—which inadequately capture the semantic depth, architectural sophistication, and long-term maintainability characteristics of developers' contributions (Kalliamvakou et al., 2016).

A principal methodological advantage of measuring time investment through work sessions rather than individual commits derives from the infeasibility of assigning uniform time allocations when only a precise initiation timestamp for each commit is observable.
Instead, we specify a temporal interval for each cluster of functionally related commits by leveraging the timestamp pair associated with the initial and terminal commits within each session.

Through systematic adjustments applied to interval boundaries and appropriate treatment of outliers, we construct weekly working time estimates capturing each user's aggregate effort investment across the observation period.
We derive raw session duration by incorporating two temporal buffers—an initial 15-minute pre-commit buffer and a subsequent 30-minute post-commit buffer—to the specified commit interval.

Subsequently, we implement heuristic adjustment protocols including discounting isolated singleton commits and recalibrating anomalously high-frequency commit sequences that likely reflect automated processes rather than sustained human coding effort.

At present, there is no consensus on how to map style-based text metrics like readability or function length onto operational performance or maintainability, and because such metrics are highly endogenous to individual and team coding conventions, we rely instead on outcome-based measures of engineering quality.
Specifically, we focus on the proportion of code rewritten shortly after submission (Code Churn %) and the proportion of duplicated, non-keyword lines (Copy-pasted %).
These indicators directly reflect technical debt accumulation and maintenance burden while remaining predominantly agnostic to stylistic conventions across developers and programming environments.
Our study has several limitations that suggest important directions for future research.

First, we focus on CX, a specific ACA whose capabilities and user experience differ from earlier versions such as the initial Copilot release; future work should compare initial releases and subsequent updates as these tools evolve.

Second, our observation window captures only immediate and short-run responses, so we cannot examine whether casual developers who initially expand their breadth eventually deepen their engagement, whether observed quality trade-offs persist as users gain experience, or whether more experienced developers converge to similar patterns as they become accustomed to AI-generated suggestions; future longitudinal studies that track developers' behavior and code quality over longer horizons would be valuable to address these dynamics.

Third, our empirical setting is limited to public GitHub repositories, and while we expect our mechanisms to be relevant beyond OSS, future work is needed to assess whether similar patterns arise in proprietary development environments with different incentive structures, oversight, and support resources.

Fourth, our intent-to-treat design relies on Pro status as a proxy for CX access; although robustness checks support this choice, studies with more direct measures of generative AI usage could sharpen identification.

Finally, while our LinkedIn data reveal suggestive associations with short-run career benefits, longer-horizon and business-cycle-spanning data are needed to understand how AI-assisted development shapes developers' careers over time.

Fourth, our intent-to-treat design relies on Pro status as a proxy for CX access; although robustness checks support this choice, studies with more direct measures of generative AI usage could sharpen identification.

While these results demonstrate that CX access correlates positively with enhanced internal career mobility, the precise causal mechanism (whether operating through enahnced signaling value of observable OSS participation or through accelerated skill acquisition enabling superior task performance) remains an open empirical question requiring further investigation.
However, we lack empirical means to validate these conjectures with our data, so our findings are best understood as evidence about open source development activity.

We therefore view our results as providing indicative evidence into how ACAs may reconfigure work patterns in organizational settings, contingent on the institutional and governance differences that developers in such environments necessarily confront.
However, these opportunities come with significant quality and sustainability considerations.

The increase in copy-pasted code and reduced novelty among casual contributors suggest that maintainers and organizations may need stronger review routines and tooling that prioritize contribution distinctiveness and maintainability alongside volume.
These patterns align with recent evidence that AI-assisted programming may increase maintenance burdens over time (Xu et al., 2025), highlighting a critical limitation.
While AI tools demonstrably help developers engage with more projects and increase output, they do not substitute for experienced developers in producing maintainable, innovative solutions.
This asymmetry creates a potential organizational dilemma.

Organizations facing short-term productivity gains from AI-augmented junior work may be tempted to adjust hiring strategies—retaining senior developers while reducing junior recruitment on the assumption that AI can substitute for entry-level contributions.

However, such strategies risk disrupting traditional junior-to-senior progression paths through which developers historically acquired the architectural expertise and system design capabilities that remain difficult for AI to replicate.

The eventual departure or retirement of senior developers from organizations that have curtailed junior hiring could compound the maintenance and technical debt challenges we document, leaving organizations with insufficient experienced personnel to manage increasingly complex codebases.

notes: This is an empirical study using a natural experiment design (difference-in-differences with coarsened exact matching) to examine how GitHub Copilot X (CX) affects open source software developers' work patterns. The paper analyzes a 27-week panel of 1,350 GitHub developers and supplements this with LinkedIn career trajectory data for 470 developers. The study finds that CX increases commit frequency (~12%) and active repositories (~6%), with heterogeneous effects by developer experience level. Casual developers show larger increases in work hours (~48%) and language exploration (~68%), while experienced developers show minimal changes. The paper documents trade-offs: while productivity increases, there are increases in copy-pasted code (~2 percentage points) and declines in project novelty for casual users. Career analysis shows CX access is associated with higher rates of internal transitions and promotions within 6-12 months. The paper includes extensive robustness checks (parallel trends tests, alternative matching specifications, AI code detection validation) and supplementary analyses (GitHub discussion sentiment analysis, heterogeneity by student status and OSS collaboration propensity, mediation analysis).
